title: Secrets hardcoded in environment variables
description: Plaintext secrets in env declarations of docker-compose or Kubernetes manifests are visible to anyone with read access to the repository or the container runtime environment inspection.
remediation: Use Kubernetes Secrets with secretKeyRef, Docker secrets, or an external secrets manager (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager). Never commit plaintext secrets to source control.

title: .env file committed to repository
description: A .env file containing secrets has been committed to the repository. These files are often not encrypted and remain in git history even after deletion, allowing extraction of credentials.
remediation: Add .env* to .gitignore immediately. Rotate all exposed credentials. Use .env.example with placeholder values for documentation. Store real secrets in a vault or CI/CD secret store.

title: Terraform variable with secret default value
description: Terraform variables for sensitive values have default values hardcoded in configuration. These defaults are stored in state files and version control, exposing credentials.
remediation: Mark sensitive variables with sensitive = true and never set defaults for secrets. Use terraform.tfvars (gitignored), environment variables (TF_VAR_*), or a secrets manager data source.

title: Inline secrets in docker-compose environment
description: Docker Compose files contain plaintext secrets in environment variable declarations. These values are visible in the repository, in container inspect output, and in process listings.
remediation: Use Docker secrets or environment variable references: environment: { DB_PASSWORD: ${DB_PASSWORD} }. For Docker Swarm, use the secrets: top-level key with external: true.
